title: Build item->agent matcher never binds implementation items to the generated python-implementer; every impl item falls back to general-purpose even after equip
evidence:
build next [mcp-server]: #9 `app/core/mcp/server.py` — `build_mcp_server` constructs `FastMCP(...)` and registers tools + resources
  agent: general-purpose (fallback)
  subagent_type: general-purpose ... [assistant:] "the build's deterministic matcher still maps item #9 to `general-purpose` — the tuned agents exist now, but this particular item's text didn't bind to `python-implementer`'s capability keywords (server-wiring isn't a clean match)"
what_happened: After /dummyindex-equip wrote python-implementer/python-tester/backend-reviewer and equipment.json (schema v2), `dummyindex context build --next` STILL returned `agent: general-purpose (fallback)` for every implementation item: #9, #10, #13, #14, #15, #18, #19, #20, #22, #23, #28 (11 post-equip items; 21 total fallback outputs across the session). Only test items (#17, #21, #24, #25 -> python-tester) and #26 (-> backend-reviewer) ever bound. Claude compensated by manually dispatching subagent_type=python-implementer for each impl item, ignoring the CLI's mapping — i.e. the deterministic routing layer that justifies equip contributed nothing for the dominant item type.
root_cause_guess: The capability-keyword matcher in build's item->equipment resolution is too narrow: python-implementer's capability keywords (likely 'implement', stack words) don't match checklist items phrased as file-path + contract descriptions ('app/core/mcp/server.py — build_mcp_server constructs ...'). Tester/reviewer keywords ('tests', 'Docs') match incidentally. Matcher needs a default 'code-change items -> stack implementer' rule rather than keyword binding.

title: Equip adopts 'Frontend Developer' registry specialist as the covering specialist for a pure-backend FastAPI repo and a security/auth-heavy MCP proposal
evidence: equip plan (--dry-run, nothing written): ... adopt   agent  Frontend Developer  ->  (registry specialist) ... [assistant:] "One oddity worth flagging: it wants to **adopt \"Frontend Developer\"** as the covering specialist for this proposal — surprising for a security/auth-heavy *backend* MCP port (a Security specialist would fit better). ... The catalog made that call from the plan text; I won't override it." ... "Frontend Developer adopted as the proposal's covering specialist — manifest-only (empty `grounded_in`, no file). As flagged, it's a poor cover for a backend/auth port; I won't route MCP work to it"
what_happened: `dummyindex context equip --for-proposal mcp-server` on a repo detected as stack=python frameworks=['FastAPI'] (no frontend at all) chose 'Frontend Developer' from the registry as the gap-covering specialist for an MCP server/auth/JWT proposal. It landed in equipment.json with subagent_type='Frontend Developer' and grounded_in=[]. Claude explicitly flagged it as a wrong match and never used it — dead/wrong wiring in the manifest.
root_cause_guess: Coverage-gap resolution scores registry specialists by naive keyword overlap with plan text (MCP tools serving 'clients building UI' / design-tokens wording likely matched 'frontend'), and/or the documented 'frontend stays adopt-fallback' default fires regardless of detected stack. No stack-consistency check (python/FastAPI backend should exclude frontend adoption) and no security-specialist mapping for auth/JWT-heavy proposals.

title: Build ran the first 9 items on general-purpose fallback because plan didn't auto-equip and build neither stopped nor suggested equipping
evidence:
build next [mcp-server]: #0 `fastmcp` (v2) added to `pyproject.toml`...
  agent: general-purpose (fallback)
  subagent_type: general-purpose ... [assistant, when user finally ran /dummyindex-equip mid-build:] "the build CLI has been falling back to `general-purpose` because there's no `.context/equipment.json` — equipping will generate project-tuned implementer/tester/reviewer agents so the remaining items map to better-fitted agents."
what_happened: The /dummyindex-plan (v0.15.0) flow produced the proposal without equipping the repo, and /dummyindex-build silently dispatched general-purpose agents for items #0–#8. Only when the user manually invoked /dummyindex-equip mid-build (after 9/42 items) did tuned agents exist. The unequipped state was never surfaced as a warning by the CLI or skill — the user had to know to equip.
root_cause_guess: v0.15.0 plan/build skills had no equip integration: plan didn't run `equip apply --for-proposal` and build had no 'not equipped -> stop and warn' gate (both were added in later versions, confirming the gap). The CLI's silent fallback hides the missing toolkit.

title: Docs-writing checklist item mapped to backend-reviewer; Claude overrode the CLI mapping with general-purpose
evidence: build next ... #26 agent=backend-reviewer | Docs updated: `.context/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` (mount + `core...` ... [actual dispatch:] {"description": "Update docs for MCP feature", "subagent_type": "general-purpose", "prompt": "Update this backend's canonical docs to record the newly-built MCP server feature... This is DOC-WRITING (targeted additions), not code."}
what_happened: The one item the matcher confidently bound to a tuned non-tester agent (#26, a documentation-update task) was bound to the review agent backend-reviewer — a critic persona, not a writer. Claude judged it a mis-fit and dispatched general-purpose instead. So in this session the CLI's mapping was overridden by the orchestrating model in BOTH directions (impl items: fallback->implementer; docs item: reviewer->general-purpose).
root_cause_guess: Keyword 'Docs'/'reviewed' style matching binds doc tasks to the reviewer; v0.15.0 has no docs specialist (added later in v0.17.0), and the matcher has no notion of agent role (writer vs critic).

title: User asked for an 'llm council' — Claude hand-rolled an ad-hoc workflow instead of dummyindex's council/audit machinery
evidence: USER: "i need you to invoke an llm coucil and debate the db setup" ... ASSISTANT: "The user explicitly asked for a multi-agent council — that's a clear opt-in to orchestration. I'll convene a 5-member expert council..." ... later USER: "can you log these concerns in correct places"
what_happened: Despite the repo having a full dummyindex .context with council artifacts (features/*/council/01-dev-draft.md etc.) and dummyindex owning the 'council' vocabulary, the request was satisfied with a bespoke background Workflow (11 agents, ~1M tokens). It worked, but its findings landed only in chat/tmp output; the user then had to separately ask for them to be logged 'in correct places', which the assistant did by hand-editing BACKLOG.md/DECISIONS.md/SCHEMA.md. None of it flowed through dummyindex's council/concerns.md pipeline.
root_cause_guess: The 0.15.0 install shipped no on-demand audit/council entry point in the repo (dummyindex-audit came later), and the council trigger phrases didn't route anywhere, so 'council' requests bypass dummyindex entirely and results must be manually folded into .context.

title: Council subagent reported the dummyindex CLI 'not installed in this environment' and bypassed atomic placement
evidence: The `dummyindex context conventions-write` CLI is not installed in this environment; the file was written directly to `/mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/frontend/.context/conventions/data-access.md`.
what_happened: One of four parallel conventions-author subagents could not find the dummyindex binary on its PATH (the other three succeeded), so it wrote the conventions file directly instead of via the CLI's atomic conventions-write. The orchestrator noticed and defensively re-placed the file through the CLI afterwards. Silent improvised fallback could have left malformed/unsectioned conventions docs.
root_cause_guess: Subagent shells don't always inherit the uv-tool PATH where dummyindex is installed; council persona prompts mandate CLI use but include no PATH guard or absolute-binary fallback, so dispatched agents quietly route around the tool.

title: Reality-check resolves bare-basename citations against the wrong file, producing 21 false contradictions
evidence:
=== assets-resources
- ``__init__.py:98`` (plan.md) — file has 0 line(s), claim cites line 98
- ``__init__.py:574`` (plan.md) — file has 0 line(s), claim cites line 574 ... [final report:] "Honesty note: the initial run flagged 21 \"contradictions\" — all were bare-basename citations (`__init__.py:152`) the checker resolved against the wrong file, plus one stdlib-API claim. Fixed by fully-qualifying citations (verified against source, none factually wrong)"
what_happened: `dummyindex context reality-check --demote` flagged 22 'contradicted' claims across 8 of 11 features. Every single one was a false positive: the checker resolved citations like `__init__.py:152` against some other (often empty) file with the same basename instead of the file named in surrounding prose. Remediation cost: a citation-hygiene subagent (79 tool uses, 84.8k tokens, 6.5 min), a second micro-agent for one straggler (`router.py:128`), and three reality-check re-runs before reaching 172/172 verified. Recurs in every feature with files named __init__.py/router.py/enums.py/schemas.py — i.e., any Python repo.
root_cause_guess: The claim extractor stores only the basename as `subject` and resolves it via a naive (possibly first-match or wrong-dir) lookup in map/symbols.json instead of disambiguating via the feature's `files` list or the surrounding prose; packages full of identically-named __init__.py/router.py files break it.

title: Reality-check misparses stdlib/external-API references as repo-symbol claims; docs had to be reworded to dodge the parser
evidence: "- ``_LlmClient._ensure_initialised()` uses `os.environ.setdefault`` (plan.md) — symbol object not found in map/symbols.json" ... orchestrator's fix instruction: "rephrase to avoid the `` `X` uses `Y` `` backtick pattern for non-repo symbols — e.g. \"`_LlmClient._ensure_initialised()` seeds provider keys via the stdlib os.environ.setdefault call\" (no backticks around the stdlib reference). Meaning must not change."
what_happened: A factually correct doc sentence citing the stdlib (`os.environ.setdefault`) was extracted as a repo-symbol relationship claim and marked contradicted because the symbol isn't in map/symbols.json. The sanctioned fix was to deliberately contort the doc's markdown formatting (drop backticks) so the checker's pattern wouldn't fire — degrading docs to satisfy the tool. The stage-4 brief even started warning authors that 'bare names break' the checker.
root_cause_guess: Claim extraction is a backtick-pattern regex with no allowlist/check for non-repo (stdlib/third-party) symbols before classifying a claim as verifiable-against-symbols.json.

title: Structural review flags splits the tooling cannot apply — oversized mixed features ship in the final index
evidence: "merges: 0 | splits flagged: 7" ... dispatch brief: "splits are surfaced as flags only — they can't be applied automatically; note them" ... final report: "**7 split flags** the structural review couldn't auto-apply (e.g. `workspace-org-core` spans 4 subdomains) — in `.context/features/_structural-plan.json` for human review"
what_happened: The architect's structural review found 7 communities spanning multiple distinct subdomains (workspace-org-core alone is 210 members / 26 files across 4 subdomains), but the CLI offers `features-rename` and `features-merge` with no `features-split` verb, so all 7 were deferred as flags. The committed index permanently carries known-overgrouped features whose docs blend unrelated domains.
root_cause_guess: Missing CLI verb: splitting requires re-partitioning members/files/flows across two feature folders plus JSON ref updates, which was never implemented; the skill papers over the gap by declaring splits 'flags only'.

title: No CLI path to re-council drifted-but-already-enriched features; council-batch frontier omits them and confidence must be hand-edited
evidence: "council-batch surfaces un-enriched features (`icon-catalog`, `utils-*`), not my drifted ones — its frontier is the per-feature council log, so the already-enriched-but-drifted features need explicit scoped re-council." ... "To re-council the drifted features I need to re-open their council logs. Let me find the reset mechanism" ... "Now: flip `icon-catalog` to INFERRED (no CLI for it; it's genuinely enriched now with full council/ dir)"
what_happened: The core task — fold 5 drifted features back through the council — is unsupported by the CLI. `council-batch --next` only surfaces features with no council log (it offered icon-catalog plus trivial utils-* stubs instead). Claude had to hand-orchestrate the whole pipeline: manually dispatch dev/architect/critic subagents per drifted feature, manually `cp` plan.md to council/01-dev-draft.md, and manually edit feature.json to flip confidence EXTRACTED→INFERRED because no verb exists to mark a feature enriched or reset its log.
root_cause_guess: The council frontier is keyed solely on the per-feature council log, with no awareness of the reconcile report's drifted_features; missing verbs: a council-log reset/re-open, a confidence/mark-enriched setter, and a `--recouncil <feature>` CLI primitive matching what the skill docs reference.

title: Inconsistent council state files: enriched features missing .hash baselines; INDEX.json schema tripped naive parsing
evidence:
=== .hash files for drifted features ===
assets-resources: hash=3666c16bf1b69ace... confidence=INFERRED
...
mcp-server: NO .hash  confidence=INFERRED
icon-catalog: NO .hash  confidence=EXTRACTED
[separately]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
    ... json.load(open('.context/features/INDEX.json')) ...
KeyError: 'id'
what_happened: While reconstructing enrichment state, Claude found mcp-server (fully enriched, INFERRED) had no `.hash` source-baseline file — the artifact the documented resume logic uses to detect drift — and icon-catalog likewise. Separately, a first attempt to read features/INDEX.json failed with KeyError because the entry schema doesn't use an 'id' key, requiring a second probing pass. Both are small but forced extra forensic turns.
root_cause_guess: Hash baselines are written by some pipeline paths but not others (e.g., features arriving via merge or older versions), and the JSON index schemas are undocumented, so even the orchestrating agent must reverse-engineer them per session.

title: council-batch --next cannot be scoped to drifted features — forces whole-repo council, contradicting the reconcile doc
evidence: "`council-batch --next` walks the frontier across **all** features (it's the unscoped, whole-repo path — note it pulled in chat, brain, projects, etc. which didn't change). The reconcile doc is explicit that I should scope to the drifted features only: *\"Always pass the feature id. Bare --recouncil re-runs the whole repo — the opposite of a scoped reconcile.\"*" ... "`council-batch` has no `--feature` scoping — it's whole-repo only, and the logs show stage 2 was never logged complete across the repo, so that loop would re-council all 13 features."
what_happened: During the user-requested recouncil of 4 drifted features, the documented batch loop (`council-batch --next`) returned dispatch units for 8 unrelated, unchanged features (chat, brain, projects, home, account...). The skill's own reconcile doc demands scoping, but the CLI primitive it points at has no --feature flag, and council-log state was stale (stage 2 never logged complete repo-wide). The agent abandoned the CLI loop and hand-orchestrated 12 scoped agent dispatches manually.
root_cause_guess: Mismatch between council/65-reconcile.md (scoped reconcile) and the council-batch CLI surface (whole-repo frontier only, no per-feature filter); council-log completion state also doesn't survive/reflect prior sessions, so the frontier restarts from scratch.

title: reality-check files false contradictions on doc/manifest citations and auto-demotes features to AMBIGUOUS with no promote path
evidence: "=== reality-check: tests === ## Contradicted ... - ``package.json:20`` (plan.md) — file not found - ``package.json:14`` (concerns.md) — file not found" ... "shared-lib: feature.json confidence = AMBIGUOUS ... tests: feature.json confidence = AMBIGUOUS" ... "`mark-enriched` only clears the pending marker; there's no explicit confidence setter, and reality-check only *demotes*, never promotes."
what_happened: Fresh, correct council docs cited `spec.md:15` and `package.json:14/:20` (legitimate references to a doc line and npm scripts); reality-check --demote treated these as AST-verifiable source claims, reported "file not found", and demoted shared-lib and tests to confidence AMBIGUOUS in feature.json and INDEX.json. There is no CLI command to restore confidence, so the agent hand-edited feature.json + INDEX.json back to EXTRACTED and reworded the docs to strip the path:line cites before re-running reality-check clean.
root_cause_guess: reality-check's claim extractor treats every `path:line` citation as a source-code claim, but its file resolver can't find non-indexed files (package.json, the spec.md doc itself); demote-on-contradiction is one-way with no corresponding promote/undo verb.

title: equip-generated agent files are not formatter-stable — repo prettier flips them to user-modified
evidence: "user-modified frontend-docs-specialist  (v1.0.0) user-modified frontend-security-specialist  (v1.0.0)" ... "Root cause found: equip's render isn't prettier-stable, and the frontend's prettier hook formats `.md` — so every commit flips equip agents to `user-modified`. (The backend avoids this entirely: it uses `ruff`, which never touches `.md`.)" ... "=== does prettier WANT to change the pristine render? === [warn] Code style issues found in 2 files."
what_happened: After `equip add-specialist security|docs` succeeded, committing the new agents through the repo's pre-commit prettier hook reformatted the .md files, so `equip status` classified them user-modified vs their origin-hash baseline. Even a fresh `equip reset` render immediately failed `prettier --check`, threatening a permanent reset↔prettier loop. The fix had to be done outside dummyindex: adding `.claude/` paths to .prettierignore.
root_cause_guess: equip's hash-baseline integrity check assumes byte-identical files post-generation, but the rendered markdown isn't normalized to common formatter styles; equip neither formats its output with the repo's detected formatter nor excludes its artifacts from format hooks it knows about (it even wires a PostToolUse formatter hook itself).

title: council-batch --next cannot be scoped to target features — unusable for a scoped recouncil
evidence:
council-batch: stage 1 — 2 parallel units (dispatch concurrently, barrier, then re-run --next):
  utils-test-conftest: dev → SubagentType.AI [AI]
  utils-typing: dev → SubagentType.AI [AI] // Claude: "`council-batch --next` is tracking *global* council state (it surfaced `utils-test-conftest`/`utils-typing`, not my targets) — so it's not the right driver for a **scoped** recouncil."
what_happened: When the user chose 'Run recouncil now' for the two features the build touched, Claude tried the documented council driver `council-batch --next`, but it surfaced two unrelated features from global council state with no flag to scope it. Claude abandoned the CLI driver and hand-orchestrated all three stages (manual council-log start/complete calls, manual `cp` snapshots of dev drafts) — error-prone plumbing the CLI was supposed to own.
root_cause_guess: council-batch only iterates the global pending-council queue; it lacks a --feature/--only filter mirroring `--recouncil <feature>` semantics.

title: build --next-wave keyword-matches a docs specialist to a code-implementation task
evidence: "index": 0, "text": "`list_for_brand` gains `variant: str | None = None` + ... outer-query-unaliased comment added (`app/core/resources/assets/reads.py:42-116`)", "agent": "backend-docs-specialist" // Claude: "One override: the CLI keyword-matched item 0 to `backend-docs-specialist` (triggered by \"comment added\"), but it's a code-implementation task. I'll dispatch all three with `python-implementer` and note it."
what_happened: Wave 1's first checklist item — a SQL/service code change — was deterministically routed to backend-docs-specialist because the item text contained the phrase 'comment added'. The conductor caught it and manually overrode to python-implementer; a less careful run would have dispatched a docs persona to write production SQL.
root_cause_guess: The build verb's agent resolution uses naive keyword matching over checklist text against specialist capability keywords ('comment'/'doc' → docs), with no precedence for the file-path/code-shaped signal or honor for plan-time '— via' tags on that item.

title: council-batch frontier is council-log-completeness-based, so drifted features with complete logs are never re-enriched and there is no force/recouncil flag
evidence: The batch returned `utils-*` features (never-enriched) plus `guideline-roles` — but **not** the 6 drifted features. The council-batch frontier keys off council-log *completeness*, and the drifted features already have complete logs, so they're considered done despite the content drift. I need the `--recouncil`/force path to re-enrich drifted features. [...] `council-batch` has no force flag — it's purely log-driven.
what_happened: The whole point of the reconcile was to re-enrich 6 features whose source had drifted, but `council-batch --next` refused to surface them (complete logs = done). With no force/reset flag, Claude abandoned the CLI's batch loop entirely: it recomputed each feature's aggregate source hash in raw python, compared against stored `.hash` files to determine real staleness (which contradicted the batch's verdict — "Decisive result — and it overturns the mtime/batch signal: recomputing **content** hashes shows the 6 git-drifted features are genuinely **STALE**"), then hand-dispatched all 24 council agents (3 stages x 8 features) outside the sanctioned mechanism.
root_cause_guess: council-batch only implements the resume/first-run frontier; hash-invalidation of completed features was deferred to 'the orchestrator' but no CLI verb exists to reset or force a feature's council state, so the resumption design contradicts the reconcile design.

title: section-write '--section security' fallback instruction caused stray security.md sibling files in half the critique agents
evidence: Security critique complete. `## Security` written to `concerns.md` (via `--section concerns`, which worked; `--section security` also wrote a sibling `security.md`); raw findings snapshotted to `council/10-critiques.md`. [...orchestrator cleanup:] === remove stray security.md siblings (canonical is concerns.md) ===
what_happened: The stage-3 critique prompts (derived from the council docs) told agents to use `--section concerns` with `--section security` as a fallback. At least 3–4 of the 8 security-critic agents executed the fallback even though the primary path succeeded, and the CLI happily created non-canonical `security.md` files next to concerns.md. The orchestrator had to sweep all 8 feature dirs and delete the strays before verification; one agent also self-reported removing one mid-run.
root_cause_guess: Ambiguous fallback wording in the council/skill docs plus a section-write CLI that accepts arbitrary section names and creates new sibling files instead of erroring or canonicalising to concerns.md.

title: Plan critique panel dispatched nonexistent subagent type 'Code Reviewer'
evidence: Agent type 'Code Reviewer' not found. Available agents: Accessibility Auditor, ... Backend Architect, canvas-to-code:canvas-to-code-auditor, ... frontend-reviewer, general-purpose, ... typescript-implementer, typescript-tester, ...
what_happened: During /dummyindex-plan's lightweight critique panel, the agent dispatched three critics in parallel; the risk critic used subagent_type 'Code Reviewer', which doesn't exist in the installed agent roster (the other two guesses, 'Backend Architect' and 'Test Results Analyzer', happened to exist). The dispatch failed and had to be re-run with general-purpose ('the skill says fall back to general-purpose'), costing a round-trip.
root_cause_guess: The dummyindex-plan SKILL.md names critic personas without instructing the model to validate against the actual installed agent list first; equipped-repo specialists (frontend-reviewer) weren't suggested for the critic roles either.

title: council-batch --next reports already-complete features as pending, misses content-stale ones, and leaks a Python enum repr in JSON output
evidence: council-batch --next output: '{"complete": false, "stage": 1, ... "feature_id": "utils-test-conftest", "stage": 1, "role": "dev", "subagent_type": "SubagentType.AI", "framework": "AI"}' — Claude: "The two 'incomplete' ones (`utils-test-conftest`, `utils-typing`) are trivial single-file features completed via the **stage-0 consolidation** pass — done, not pending." Memory note: "a source-changed feature with a complete log will NOT appear in the batch".
what_happened: When scoping --recouncil work, council-batch --next surfaced two trivial features that were already finished (their stage-0 consolidation isn't recorded the way the batch reader expects) while showing none of the 12 genuinely content-stale features (it is log-based, not hash-based). Claude had to ignore the batch output and compute the stale set itself. The JSON also serializes subagent_type as the raw enum repr 'SubagentType.AI' instead of a usable agent name.
root_cause_guess: council-batch derives pending work solely from _council-log.json completeness; stage-0 consolidation writes a log shape it misreads, and hash-invalidation is left to the orchestrator. The subagent_type field is str(enum) instead of enum.value.

title: Plan flow has no quality gate of its own — a self-contradictory plan.md task only got caught by the external harness advisor
evidence: **1. Fix the garbled sentence in `plan.md` task 5 — blocks (it's a defect in the artifact).** You wrote: > "Strip the `_deprecated`/`$schema`/`name`/`version`/`description`/`transitions` metadata keys are **kept** in storage..." That's self-contradictory ("Strip the … keys are kept").
what_happened: Claude wrote a garbled, both-strip-and-keep instruction into plan.md and was about to declare the proposal done. The catch came from Claude Code's built-in `advisor` server tool ('Three good catches'), not from anything dummyindex provides — the 0.15.0 plan skill has no critique panel and the CLI does no artifact validation, so on a harness without the advisor this defect would have shipped into the execution surface that build agents follow literally.
root_cause_guess: dummyindex-plan 0.15.0 ends at 'write the three docs and report' with no review round; the critique panel added in later versions exists precisely for this, but this repo's wiring never got it (see staleness issue).

title: council-batch stage 2 wants to re-run architect over ALL 14 features — pre-0.24 enrichment stage completions not recognized, forcing Claude to abandon the --recouncil loop
evidence: "Stage 2 returns **all 14 features** — not the 3 I refreshed. That's the cache problem resurfacing: the council log has no stage-2/stage-3 completion records for any feature (the original enrichment's stage completions aren't recognized by 0.24.0's council-batch), so the frontier wants to run the architect + critic across everything. Continuing would **clobber every feature's `plan.md`** (including brand-hub's hand-merged plan and home-v3's just-authored one) via ~28 more dispatches — runaway churn driven by missing logs, not real need. I'm stopping the loop here."
what_happened: During the user-requested /dummyindex --recouncil, stage 1 correctly scoped to 3 changed features, but advancing the loop (council-batch --next) returned architect units for all 14 features (brand-hub, chat, brain, ... home-v3). Claude aborted the documented council loop mid-pipeline to avoid clobbering curated plan.md files, leaving stages 2-3 permanently unrun. The recouncil therefore never completed as designed.
root_cause_guess: Council-log/stage-completion records written by an older CLI version (index stamped 0.19.2) are not migrated or recognized by 0.24.0's council-batch frontier, so per-stage dedup fails for everything enriched before the upgrade.

title: Disjoint drift signals (council-batch frontier vs reconcile --json) led Claude to misdiagnose 'corrupted hash cache', skip the requested recouncil, and user re-issued the command
evidence: "The council-batch frontier returned `projects, home, design-system-base` — features **not** in the reconcile drift list. That's a tell that the hash cache was invalidated by the earlier install/revert churn, so `council-batch` now thinks *everything* needs re-councilling. Blindly following it would rewrite all 13 features' docs… The reliable signal is `reconcile --json`."
what_happened: First /dummyindex --recouncil: reconcile --json said drifted_features = [brain, brand-hub, chat, shared-ui-custom, tests] while council-batch flagged [projects, home, design-system-base(, home-v3)] — disjoint sets. Claude distrusted council-batch, declared the cache corrupted (later self-corrected: "The hash cache is actually working"), and substituted its own scoped reconcile instead of the recouncil. The user then typed /dummyindex --recouncil a second time to get the actual recouncil run.
root_cause_guess: Two independent staleness signals (content-hash council cache vs commit-anchored reconcile delta) with no documentation of how/why they diverge; the skill gives no rule for arbitrating them, so the orchestrating model improvises and the user has to repeat the command.

title: council-batch --json leaks Python enum repr: "subagent_type": "SubagentType.FRONTEND"
evidence: "units": [ { "feature_id": "projects", "stage": 1, "role": "dev", "subagent_type": "SubagentType.FRONTEND", "framework": "React" }, …
what_happened: The machine-readable batch output emits the unserialized enum repr instead of the resolved subagent name (`dev-pick` separately returns "Frontend Developer"). Claude's first parse also printed feature names as None (used 'feature' vs actual 'feature_id'), and it had to call dev-pick per feature anyway to get a dispatchable subagent_type — the --json contract isn't directly usable for Task dispatch.
root_cause_guess: str(enum) serialization bug in the council-batch JSON encoder plus undocumented/unstable field names in the JSON schema.

title: Equip-generated agent file (.claude/agents/frontend-performance-specialist.md) surfaces as 'unassigned new files' drift requiring manual placement
evidence: unassigned new files: … .claude/agents/frontend-performance-specialist.md, app/(dashboard)/brand-hub/guidelines/[docId]/page.tsx, app/(dashboard)/home-v3/page.tsx, …
what_happened: The reconcile delta repeatedly listed an equip-generated specialist agent file among unassigned source files; Claude had to assign-files it into the 'tooling' feature to clear the drift report. dummyindex's own generated tooling pollutes its own reconcile workflow.
root_cause_guess: Reconcile's new-file scan doesn't exclude (or auto-assign) dummyindex/equip-managed paths like .claude/agents/**, so every equip render creates placement chores.

title: equip apply output (equipment.json + generated agents) repeatedly caused git checkout/stash/cherry-pick conflicts
evidence: "error: Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten by checkout: .context/equipment.json Please commit your changes or stash them before you switch branches. Aborting" ... then "Auto-merging .context/equipment.json CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in .context/equipment.json" ... later "AA .claude/agents/frontend-security-specialist.md UU .context/equipment.json error: Committing is not possible because you have unmerged files."
what_happened: Three separate git operations in one session were blocked or conflicted by equip artifacts: branch checkout aborted on a dirty equipment.json, the stash-pop of the equip output conflicted, and a cherry-pick of the proposal commits hit AA/UU conflicts on equipment.json + frontend-security-specialist.md. The assistant's resolution each time was checkout --ours/--theirs and re-running `equip apply` to regenerate ("equip: wrote 8 item(s)" — it rewrites all items unconditionally).
root_cause_guess: equip writes machine-generated, branch-divergent content (hashes/manifest entries) into tracked files with no merge-friendly format and leaves the tree dirty immediately after apply; rewriting all 8 items on every apply maximizes diff churn.

title: Equip-generated specialist agents never dispatched natively — build inlined their rules into general-purpose prompts
evidence: "Emitting all 5 slices via subagents, sequential (each depends on the previous), zero commits. Reading the project-tuned implementer's definition to inline its rules:" — every Agent dispatch in the session (critique panel, gates 3/5b/6/7, slices 1-5, Wave-4 reconciliation) used subagent_type general-purpose (one used the unrelated 'Test Results Analyzer'); prompts begin "You are the **typescript-implementer**..."
what_happened: equip generated .claude/agents/typescript-implementer.md, frontend-reviewer.md, specialists, etc. in frontend/, and dummyindex-build promises to 'dispatch tuned agents'. In practice none were available as subagent types: Claude read each agent file with head/Read and pasted a paraphrase into general-purpose Task prompts, losing the agents' tool restrictions and model pinning. ~12 dispatches in the session, all degraded this way.
root_cause_guess: Agents written into a submodule's .claude/agents aren't registered when the session roots at the mono parent; the build skill neither checks agent availability nor instructs how to dispatch a named project agent, so the equip toolkit's core value silently degrades.

title: equip cannot generate a file-backed specialist agent — user's core request unfulfillable via the documented path
evidence: User: "can you add more agents including db manager?" ... Assistant: "So the gap is real: equip **cannot** give you a file-backed DB agent. Its catalog has no \"generate a DB agent\" template, and the skill bars me from hand-authoring one *within the equip flow*. But I can step outside equip and write you a normal, editable project agent file"
what_happened: The user asked equip to add more agents including a db manager. Equip's only mechanism was adopting the fixed-registry 'Data Engineer' as a manifest-only pointer (no file). After a long exploration (dry-runs, --for-proposal scoping, help-text spelunking), Claude concluded the gap is real and stepped OUTSIDE equip to hand-write .claude/agents/db-manager.md as an unmanaged user file — exactly what the skill prose forbids ('you do not hand-author agents here'). This gap drove the entire second half of the session.
root_cause_guess: equip v0.15.x has templates only for the fixed implementer/tester/reviewer/verify quartet; capability gaps are covered solely by adopting registry pointers, with no template/codepath to GENERATE a grounded specialist file (db/security/etc.). (Memory notes this was later fixed in v0.17.0 'equip generated specialists'.)

title: Manifest-only adoption is invisible to the user — 'i dont see them under .claude'
evidence: User: "i dont see them under .claude" ... Assistant: "What is *not* a file — and never was: `Frontend Developer` (and `Data Engineer` before I trimmed it). Adopted specialists are **manifest-only by design** — they live solely as entries in `.context/equipment.json` ... That's why you don't see them under `.claude/` — there's nothing to see"
what_happened: After equip reported adopting 'Data Engineer' and 'Frontend Developer', the user looked in .claude/ and found nothing. Claude had to explain the manifest-only design twice (once after the dry-run, once after the user's complaint) and run an AskUserQuestion ('equip can't produce that (adopted = manifest-only registry pointers)') before the user got a tangible artifact via a hand-written file.
root_cause_guess: Adoption produces no visible artifact and equip's CLI output ('wrote 7 item(s)') doesn't distinguish file writes from manifest-only entries, so users reasonably expect files. UX gap: adoption should either write a file or clearly report 'manifest-only, no file created'.

title: No CLI verb to un-adopt a specialist — manifest hand-edited on user request
evidence:
User: "hand trim it" → Edit .context/equipment.json old_string: "{
 "kind": "agent",
 "name": "Data Engineer",
 "path": "",
 "source": "installed", ... "subagent_type": "Data Engineer",
 "version": null,
 "origin_hash": null
 }," new_string: ""
what_happened: The user wanted the just-adopted Data Engineer entry removed. The equip lifecycle exposes status/refresh/reset/uninstall/patch, but nothing to remove a single adopted entry (uninstall removes everything). The user re-invoked /dummyindex-equip then told Claude to 'hand trim it', and Claude deleted the JSON block from the tool-managed manifest with a raw Edit, then manually re-validated the JSON with python.
root_cause_guess: Equip lifecycle verbs only cover generated, origin-hashed items; adopted manifest entries have no add/remove granularity (no `equip unadopt <name>` / `equip adopt --remove`), forcing hand edits to a file the tool owns.

title: equip --for-proposal apply rewrites ALL generated files just to add one manifest adoption (and reports a misleading 'wrote 7 item(s)')
evidence:
equip: stack=python frameworks=['FastAPI']
  write   python-implementer  ->  .claude/agents/python-implementer.md
  write   python-tester  ->  .claude/agents/python-tester.md
  write   backend-reviewer  ->  .claude/agents/backend-reviewer.md
  write   backend-verify  ->  .claude/skills/backend-verify/SKILL.md
equip: wrote 7 item(s), skipped 0, preserved 0 user-modified, kept 0 evolved
what_happened: The only intended change was adopting Data Engineer into the manifest, but apply re-rendered all four generated files too. Claude explicitly worried this could regress the just-refreshed v1.1.0 content and verified afterwards: 'the version concern didn't materialize: the re-render was content-identical, so all four generated files held at their refreshed versions (no regression to undo)'. Output also says 'wrote 7 item(s)' while listing only 4 writes (counting manifest-only adoptions and the settings hook as 'written').
root_cause_guess: equip apply is monolithic — no additive/adopt-only mode — and its summary counter lumps file writes, manifest entries, and hook wiring into one 'wrote' number.

title: Proposal-scoped adoption missed the security capability despite RLS/tenant-isolation criticals in the plan
evidence: Assistant: "**'More' was exactly +1.** The catalog adopts only capability-covering specialists — it pointedly **skipped a Security specialist** despite the proposal's RLS/tenant-isolation criticals (Task 0, Task 17 `get_advisors security`). If you want security covered in the build loop too, that's a real gap I can raise."
what_happened: `equip --for-proposal brand-centric-migration` adopted only Data Engineer (database) even though the proposal's plan.md contains explicit security-critical tasks (RLS, tenant isolation, Supabase `get_advisors security`). Claude flagged this as a real coverage gap in the deterministic capability-detection.
root_cause_guess: The capability extractor over plan.md/checklist.md doesn't map RLS/tenant-isolation/advisor-security language to the 'security' capability, or the catalog's adoption threshold is too conservative — coverage resolution misses non-keyword-obvious capabilities.

title: equip status blind to manifest tampering — hand-edit of adopted entry registers no drift
evidence: Assistant: "`equip status` still shows the four generated tools **pristine** (it tracks only the origin-hashed generated items, so a hand-edit to an adopted entry doesn't register as drift)" ... "--- equip status sees no drift from the trim: --- equip status: pristine python-implementer (v1.1.0) pristine python-tester (v1..."
what_happened: After Claude hand-deleted the Data Engineer block from equipment.json, `equip status` reported everything pristine. The hash-baseline integrity story covers only generated files; the manifest itself (and adopted entries with origin_hash:null) has no integrity check, so direct edits — including corrupting ones — would go unnoticed by the lifecycle.
root_cause_guess: status classifies items by comparing file content to origin_hash; adopted entries have path:"" and origin_hash:null, and the manifest file is not itself baselined, leaving a verification hole in the 'never clobber / hash-baselined lifecycle' guarantee.

title: Claimed 'advisor' consultation has no recorded dispatch in the transcript
evidence: Assistant: "Two things I want to get right before mutating anything, so let me check with the advisor." ... [next assistant message, no Task/agent tool call anywhere in the session] ... "The advisor surfaced the key point I'd have gotten wrong: **adopted specialists are manifest-only dispatch hints, not newly-unlocked agents.**"
what_happened: Between the two statements there is no Task tool_use, no sidechain event, and no CLI call — the only tool calls in the whole session are Bash/Read/Edit/Write/AskUserQuestion. Claude narrated consulting an 'advisor' and attributed a conclusion to it, but no such consultation is recorded; the insight came from its own reasoning. Occurred once.
root_cause_guess: The equip skill's tone ('check with the advisor' style guidance, council-shaped vocabulary) primes Claude to narrate a consultation step it never actually dispatches — skill prose implies a mechanism that has no concrete invocation path at v0.15.

title: Audit persona catalog hardcodes subagent_type names that don't exist in the equipped repo, forcing Claude to improvise agent mapping
evidence: ---CATALOG ROLES--- architecture - Architecture & boundaries auditor -> Software Architect / correctness -> Code Reviewer / data-integrity -> Data Engineer / maintainability -> Code Reviewer / performance -> Performance Benchmarker / security -> Security Engineer / tests -> Test Results Analyzer ... assistant: '(Mapping catalog `subagent_type`s to this repo's available agents: architecture→Backend Architect, correctness/maintainability→backend-reviewer, security→Security Engineer, tests→Test Results Analyzer.)'
what_happened: The repo's .claude/agents contains only backend-reviewer, db-manager, python-implementer, python-tester (equip-generated), but `audit start` emitted a catalog pointing at generic personas like 'Software Architect' and 'Performance Benchmarker'. Claude silently invented a mapping — including 'Backend Architect', which is also not in the repo's roster — so audit dispatch correctness depended on harness fallback rather than the catalog. The audit still completed, but the catalog's subagent_type field was effectively dead weight.
root_cause_guess: audit catalog generation is static and not wired to equipment.json / the repo's actual agent roster; no resolution step maps personas to installed agents at scaffold time.

title: No CLI verb to register local/in-repo skills as equipment — manifest hand-edited via python
evidence:
python3 << 'EOF'
... if 'paper-design' not in names:
    d['items'].append(entry('paper-design', '.claude/skills/paper-design/SKILL.md', '.context/equipment/paper-design.md'))
if 'design-handoff' not in names:
    d['items'].append(entry('design-handoff', ...))
json.dump(d, open(p, 'w'), indent=2)
what_happened: paper-design and design-handoff are local .claude/skills, not marketplace plugins, so `equip install <plugin>@<marketplace>` doesn't apply and no other verb registers them. To make plan's `— via` routing see them, Claude wrote raw JSON entries (source 'installed', mechanism 'skill') directly into .context/equipment.json — bypassing the tool's own schema guarantees (origin_hash null, no baseline).
root_cause_guess: The equipment manifest supports source='installed' items but the CLI surface has no `equip adopt`/`equip register` verb for local tools; only marketplace installs and template-generated items have a sanctioned path.

title: Garbage 'Frontend Developer' entry polluting backend equipment.json; no CLI verb to remove a single entry
evidence:
{
  "kind": "agent",
  "name": "Frontend Developer",
  "path": "",
  "source": "installed",
  "capabilities": ["frontend"],
  "grounded_in": [],
  "subagent_type": "Frontend Developer",
  "version": null, "origin_hash": null, ... "mechanism": null
}
what_happened: The backend's equipment.json contained an adopted agent entry with an empty path, no grounding, and no mechanism — Claude called it 'an odd "Frontend Developer" entry' and a frontend-capability agent has no business in the backend manifest anyway. Removal again required a hand-rolled python JSON edit; equip only offers reset NAME (re-render) and uninstall (everything pristine), not removal of one adopted entry.
root_cause_guess: An earlier equip adopt/coverage-resolution pass recorded a registry agent by display name without validating path/grounding; combined with a missing `equip remove <name>` verb, broken adoptions persist until hand-edited.

title: Plugins shoehorned into schema as kind:'agent' with null version/origin_hash — lifecycle can't hash-verify plugin wiring
evidence: equipment.json: { "kind": "agent", "name": "canvas-to-code@canvas-to-code-marketplace", ... "subagent_type": "canvas-to-code:canvas-to-code-pm", "version": null, "origin_hash": null, ... } and { "kind": "agent", "name": "impeccable@impeccable", ... "subagent_type": null, "version": null, "origin_hash": null }
what_happened: Both marketplace plugins are recorded with kind "agent" although they are plugins (impeccable is "the `impeccable:impeccable` skill + ~23 /impecca... commands" per its own playbook, with subagent_type null). Both also carry version:null and origin_hash:null, so the hash-baselined lifecycle (status/refresh/reset "never clobbering a user edit") has nothing to baseline plugin entries against — manual edits or upstream changes are invisible to it.
root_cause_guess: Schema v3 has no 'plugin' kind and the installer doesn't compute a baseline hash or record a resolved version for marketplace items, only origin_ref.

title: dev-pick recommends a generic persona ('Frontend Developer') instead of the repo's equipped specialist agents; dispatch result never seen
evidence: dev-pick output: {"persona_id": "dev-frontend", "subagent_type": "Frontend Developer", "framework": "React", "fallbacks": ["Senior Developer", "general-purpose"]} — followed by Agent dispatch: {"description": "Re-enrich brand-hub .context docs", "subagent_type": "Frontend Developer", ...} — the transcript ends immediately after, with no tool result.
what_happened: For brand-hub re-enrichment, `dummyindex context dev-pick` returned subagent_type "Frontend Developer" — not any of the repo's equip-generated agents (typescript-implementer / typescript-tester / frontend-reviewer) registered in equipment.json. Claude dispatched the Agent with that type and the transcript ends right there with no result, no mark-enriched, and no reconcile-stamp — the reconcile loop's completion is unverified and brand-hub's .pending-enrichment marker (which blocks reconcile-stamp) was last seen still set.
root_cause_guess: dev-pick draws from a static council persona catalog disconnected from equipment.json, so its primary recommendation may not correspond to any installed subagent type; if 'Frontend Developer' isn't a registered agent the dispatch would fail outright (possibly why the session ends here, though truncation could also explain it).

title: Equipment matcher routes a pure DB/schema GATE task to python-implementer
evidence:
"text": "**GATE** — Mechanism = Supabase CLI migrations (`supabase/migrations/*.sql`, `supabase db push`; DECISIONS §15), applied/tested via the Supabase MCP on a `create_branch` branch... Decide the RLS/tenant-isolation model... Blocks all DDL."
  },
  "agent": "python-implementer",
  "subagent_type": "python-implementer",
  "fallback": false,
  "equipped": true
what_happened: `dummyindex context build --proposal brand-centric-migration --next --json` returned the checklist's first item — a database-migration gate (Supabase CLI migrations, baseline migration, RLS/tenant-isolation decision) — and matched it to the generic `python-implementer` agent with `fallback: false` (i.e. the matcher considered this a confident, equipped match). The prior session's recap shows the repo had DB-focused agents (an adopted 'Data Engineer' pointer and a user-owned `db-manager` agent), yet none of them were selected for an item that is entirely about schema/DDL/Supabase work. The user interrupted the session immediately after this result was printed, before any dispatch happened. Occurred once (session ended right after).
root_cause_guess: In v0.15.x the checklist→equipment matching is keyword/heuristic-based against equipment.json and likely defaults to the stack implementer for anything code-adjacent; user-owned/adopted specialist agents (db-manager, Data Engineer) either weren't registered in equipment.json or the matcher's coverage resolution doesn't consider capability domains (db vs code), so a DB gate falls through to the stack implementer. (Specialist generation + resolve_coverage precedence only landed in v0.17.0.)

title: Prior session: equip's generated/adopted specialists needed manual hand-trimming and a hand-written db agent; user asked to improve equip itself
evidence: away_summary: "Goal was equipping this backend repo with dummyindex tooling. I refreshed the four generated agents, adopted then hand-trimmed a Data Engineer pointer, and wrote a user-owned db-manager agent. Last I gave you a prompt to improve dummyindex's equip itself. Next: tell me how to proceed."
what_happened: The session-resume recap recorded in this transcript summarizes the immediately preceding session in this repo: equip's output was not usable as-rendered — an adopted 'Data Engineer' registry pointer had to be hand-trimmed, the database capability gap had to be filled with a manually written user-owned `db-manager` agent (equip produced nothing adequate for it), and the work ended with the user requesting a prompt to improve dummyindex's equip feature itself. This is corroborated in-session by issue #1: even after that manual work, the build matcher still didn't use the DB agents.
root_cause_guess: At v0.15.x equip only rendered the four stack agents (implementer/tester/reviewer/verify) and shallow registry pointers; no capability-specialist generation existed, so db coverage required manual authoring — and manually authored agents weren't integrated into the equipment manifest the build matcher reads.

title: Council-generated spec.md uses realistic 'ghp_...' API-key placeholder, flagged as Secret Keyword
evidence:
Secret Type: Secret Keyword
Location:    .context/features/integrations-oauth/spec.md:153

[file content at that line:]
3. `PUT /v1/integrations/<id>/credentials` with `{"api_key": "ghp_..."}` → `IntegrationCredentialsResponse` with `has_api_key: true`.
what_happened: detect-secrets flagged a Secret Keyword inside a dummyindex council-generated feature spec — an example request body using a GitHub-PAT-shaped placeholder (`"api_key": "ghp_..."`). The assistant confirmed it was a docs placeholder and it got swept into the blanket .context/ exclude.
root_cause_guess: Council prose generation writes example payloads with realistic secret prefixes and no `pragma: allowlist secret` annotation; generated docs should use clearly fake, scanner-safe placeholders.

title: Past dummyindex/tester agent sessions left dropped stashes and dangling commits on chore/dummyindex-context-index; user had to run a recovery audit
evidence:
[user:] "i need you to check all the conversations, worktrees and brnaches and confirm all the changes are on this brnach"
[git fsck:] ccabdd6 2026-06-10 On chore/dummyindex-context-index: tester-wip
390ddfc 2026-06-10 untracked files on chore/dummyindex-context-index: 7e9ae1e feat: migrate to brand-centric domain model...
24981d4 2026-06-10 On chore/dummyindex-context-index: tester-wip2
9b14d7c 2026-06-09 WIP on chore/dummyindex-context-index: 2cc0c4f WIP: MCP port with dummyIndex
[assistant:] "dangling commits left behind by past agent sessions"
what_happened: The user opened the session worried that work from prior agent conversations was lost. The audit found six unreachable stash-commits (tester-wip, tester-wip2, plus index/untracked-file stashes) dropped from a dummyindex working branch, containing real test-file edits (7 files, 66 insertions/118 deletions) and a .context/ proposal spec.md enrichment. They turned out to be superseded by a later commit only by luck of timing; the user spent a whole verification pass confirming nothing was lost. dummyindex-era work was also scattered across four branches (mcp-port, chore/dummyindex-context-index, backend-rework, development) as WIP commits ('WIP: MCP port with dummyIndex').
root_cause_guess: Equip-generated tester/implementer agent sessions ran concurrently on a shared checkout, stashing WIP under the dummyindex context branch and later dropping those stashes instead of committing — the known concurrent-worktree hazard. dummyindex's agent workflow has no rule to commit (or worktree-isolate) WIP before branch switches, so multi-session work scatters and nearly loses changes.

title: Plan-stage deferrals (critique-panel 'Decision #5: DEFERRED to v2') conflicted with user intent — user demanded 'nothing defered'
evidence: Spec (quoted by assistant): "**Decision #5:** Version history DEFERRED to v2 — 'the version feed is `kind`-scoped and can't scope to `variant=illustration`. Revisit when the backend can scope `/assets/versions` by `variant`.'" ... USER: "i need it too be 100% functional nothing defered, with ability to upload, download, view illustrations and all screens wired correctly"
what_happened: The dummyindex-plan spec (revised after its critique panel) deliberately scoped the feature to 'zero new backend surface' and deferred version history, cross-format download, and variant filtering to v2. The user clearly did not share that scoping — when asked to 'update backend' they pushed back: '100% functional nothing defered'. The deferred items became this session's entire remediation workload (and the backend plan prompt's 4 gap items).
root_cause_guess: The plan skill's critique panel optimizes for minimal scope and records deferrals as spec 'Decisions' without an explicit user sign-off step, so deferrals silently become product gaps the user discovers later; there is also no follow-up artifact (e.g. a v2 proposal stub) tracking what was deferred.

title: equip apply/add-specialist rewrites equipment.json from scratch and silently drops marketplace plugin install records
evidence: git show 696c932 -- .context/equipment.json removes: '{ "kind": "agent", "name": "superpowers@claude-plugins-official", "path": ".claude/settings.json", "source": "marketplace", ... "grounded_in": [".context/equipment/superpowers.md"], "marketplace": "claude-plugins-official", "origin_repo": "anthropics/claude-plugins-official", "mechanism": "native" }'. Current /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/backend/.context/equipment.json has no plugin entry, yet .claude/settings.json still has 'enabledPlugins: {"superpowers@claude-plugins-official": true}' and the playbook .context/equipment/superpowers.md exists.
what_happened: The superpowers plugin was installed via equip (manifest entry + usage playbook + native settings wiring all present at commit a7be5db). A later `equip add-specialist` run (commit 696c932, which added backend-docs/security specialists) rebuilt the manifest and the plugin record vanished, while the plugin stayed enabled in settings.json. Result on disk now: `dummyindex context equip status --json` lists only the 6 generated items and reports "missing_playbook": [] — the plugin manager is blind to a plugin it installed, and `equip uninstall` would leave it enabled (breaking the skill's promise: 'Every install is recorded in .context/equipment.json ... so status / uninstall cover marketplace and vendored items').
root_cause_guess: In /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/memory/dummyindex/dummyindex/cli/equip/dispatch.py `_apply_write()` builds the new manifest as `EquipmentManifest(items=tuple(written))` where `written` = rendered items + decision.adopt + hook items. Prior manifest entries with source 'marketplace' (and vendored items) are never carried forward — only generated baselines and re-derived adoptions survive a re-apply.

title: equipment.json grounded_in for backend-security-specialist points at three nonexistent files
evidence: .context/equipment.json, backend-security-specialist item: '"grounded_in": [... ".context/conventions/auth.md", ".context/conventions/security.md", ".context/DECISIONS.md"]'. On disk, .context/conventions/ contains only coding-practices.md, data-access.md, folder-organization.md, naming.json, naming.md, testing.md; ls .context/DECISIONS.md → 'No such file or directory' (the repo's real decisions doc is .context/docs/DECISIONS.md).
what_happened: The security specialist's manifest grounding records auth.md, security.md and DECISIONS.md that do not exist in this repo, and misses the actually-existing .context/docs/DECISIONS.md. The rendered agent file hedges with '(if present)', but the manifest's grounded_in — the audit trail of what the tool is grounded in — is wrong, and any tooling that trusts it (or an agent that tries to read those paths) hits dead links.
root_cause_guess: grounding_docs are hardcoded constants in /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/memory/dummyindex/dummyindex/context/domains/equip/generate/specialists.py (lines 87-97: '.context/conventions/auth.md', '.context/conventions/security.md', _DECISIONS='.context/DECISIONS.md') and are recorded into the manifest without an existence filter against the target repo.

title: equip apply --for-proposal rewrote equipment.json and silently dropped 5 previously tracked items (specialists, marketplace plugin, vendored skills)
evidence: git show 2742f514:.context/equipment.json items: [typescript-implementer, typescript-tester, frontend-reviewer, frontend-verify, frontend-docs-specialist, frontend-security-specialist, frontend-performance-specialist, prettier-format, canvas-to-code@canvas-to-code-marketplace (source=marketplace), paper-design (installed), design-handoff (installed)]. git show 3333b758:.context/equipment.json (and HEAD): docs-specialist, performance-specialist, canvas-to-code, paper-design, design-handoff are GONE; search-specialist and a second 'frontend-reviewer (installed)' appeared. Skill spec: 'a plain `equip` re-run afterward **preserves** it (never silently drops a specialist you added)' and 'Every install is recorded in `.context/equipment.json`'.
what_happened: Between commits 2742f514 and 3333b758 ('add art-direction-paper-rebuild proposal + equip toolkit'), the manifest was regenerated from scratch. The dropped items still exist live: .claude/agents/frontend-docs-specialist.md (version 1.1.0 — it had even been patch-bumped) and frontend-performance-specialist.md both carry `<!-- dummyindex:generated -->`, canvas-to-code@canvas-to-code-marketplace is enabled in .claude/settings.json, and skills paper-design/ + design-handoff/ are on disk — yet none are in equipment.json, so equip status/refresh/uninstall are now blind to them. Usage docs at .context/equipment/canvas-to-code.md and paper-design.md are orphaned, and frontend/CLAUDE.md's claim 'Both tools are registered as **dummyindex equipment**' is false against the live manifest.
root_cause_guess: equip apply (the --for-proposal path) writes the manifest as a fresh render of the current plan's coverage instead of merging with the existing manifest, losing every item the current run didn't itself produce — exactly the 'never silently drops' contract violation.

title: Duplicate conflicting 'frontend-reviewer' entries in equipment.json; adopted entry violates the path:"" contract
evidence: /mnt/windows-ssd/Projects/opensession/BOS-Mono/frontend/.context/equipment.json has two items named frontend-reviewer: one {"source": "generated", "path": ".claude/agents/frontend-reviewer.md", "version": "1.0.0", "origin_hash": "sha256:55ef..."} and one {"source": "installed", "path": ".claude/agents/frontend-reviewer.md", "capabilities": ["frontend", "review"], "origin_hash": null}. Skill spec: 'ADOPTED specialist — a manifest-only pointer (`"path": ""`, `"source": "installed"`, no `origin_hash`) ... **No file is written**'.
what_happened: The same agent file is claimed twice with conflicting lifecycle semantics — one entry says equip owns it (hash-baselined, refreshable), the other says it's an externally 'installed' agent that was adopted. The adopted entry also carries a non-empty path pointing at the generated file, breaking the documented manifest-only (`path: ""`) contract for adoptions. Lifecycle verbs (status/refresh/reset/uninstall) and capability resolution now have two contradictory records for one path.
root_cause_guess: The --for-proposal coverage resolver adopted the existing 'frontend-reviewer' to cover a 'frontend' capability gap without checking that the name/path was already a generated item in the same manifest, and filled the adoption's path from the discovered file instead of leaving it empty.

title: equipment.json grounded_in and generated specialist bodies reference four nonexistent .context files
evidence: equipment.json frontend-security-specialist grounded_in includes '.context/conventions/auth.md', '.context/conventions/security.md', '.context/DECISIONS.md'; frontend-search-specialist includes '.context/docs/SCHEMA.md', '.context/DECISIONS.md'. ls: all four 'No such file or directory'. Agent bodies repeat them: frontend-security-specialist.md:24 '2. `.context/conventions/auth.md` and `.context/conventions/security.md`'; frontend-search-specialist.md:23 '2. `.context/conventions/data-access.md` and `.context/docs/SCHEMA.md`'.
what_happened: The generated security and search specialists are 'grounded' in files dummyindex never generated for this repo (only DECISIONS.md is softened with '(if present)'; the conventions/SCHEMA references are unconditional mandatory-read steps). Agents dispatched from these specialists burn their grounding phase failing to read phantom paths, and the manifest's grounded_in — supposed to record real grounding — is partly fiction.
root_cause_guess: Specialist templates (security/search) carry a fixed grounded_in list assuming a fuller .context layout (auth/security conventions, docs/SCHEMA.md, DECISIONS.md) instead of filtering the list against files that actually exist at render time.

title: Equipment matching emits wrong subagent_type: a decision GATE and a pytest item both mapped to python-implementer
evidence: --next for GATE item #0: 'agent: python-implementer, subagent_type: python-implementer' ... later: 'NEXT #13 -> python-implementer / ... add a pytest scan'
what_happened: GATE (decision) item matched to python-implementer; explicit pytest work also matched to python-implementer (Claude overrode to python-tester). Matcher suggestions ignored whenever they mattered.
root_cause_guess: Item-to-equipment matching is keyword/coarse with no notion of item kind (gate/decision vs implement vs test).
